title: Appendicitis: teniae coli converge on the appendix
vignette: A 17-year-old girl is brought to the hospital due to abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She began having periumbilical pain and nausea several hours ago and initially attributed her symptoms to some "bad food" she ate at a potluck lunch earlier in the day. However, her pain progressively worsened and became localized to the right lower quadrant. Temperature is 101 F (38.3 C), blood pressure is 124/78 mm Hg, pulse is 92/min, and respirations are 14/min. On examination, the patient has right lower quadrant tenderness with guarding. Laboratory evaluation shows leukocytosis. Surgical intervention is planned. During surgery, which of the following landmarks is most helpful in identifying the diseased organ?

clues.note: Early periumbilical (visceral, T10) pain that later migrates is the classic opening of appendicitis - visceral afferents from the midgut appendix refer to the umbilicus before parietal peritoneal irritation localizes the pain.

clues.text: became localized to the right lower quadrant
clues.note: Migration of pain from periumbilical to McBurney's point (RLQ) as the inflamed appendix irritates the parietal peritoneum is the hallmark sequence of acute appendicitis.

clues.text: right lower quadrant tenderness with guarding
clues.note: RLQ tenderness + guarding + fever + leukocytosis confirms acute appendicitis. The surgical question then becomes how to find the appendix - by following an anatomic landmark.

clues.text: which of the following landmarks is most helpful in identifying the diseased organ
clues.note: Intraoperatively, the three teniae coli of the cecum converge at the base of the appendix. Following the teniae to their convergence reliably locates the appendiceal base regardless of tip position (retrocecal, pelvic, etc.).

whatMatters: Clinically this is appendicitis (periumbilical -> RLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis). The anatomy question: what landmark finds the appendix at surgery? The three teniae coli converge at the appendiceal base - answer E.
strategy: For 'find the appendix' anatomy, anchor on the teniae coli: these three longitudinal muscle bands run along the cecum/colon and fuse at the base of the appendix. Tracing them to their convergence locates the appendiceal base even when the tip lies in an atypical position.

eliminations.label: greater omentum - not a localizing landmark for the appendix base
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the greater omentum is an apron of fat hanging from the greater curvature of the stomach; it can wall off an inflamed appendix. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it does not point to the appendiceal base and is not the reliable surgical landmark. ✅ Correct if: asked what limits/walls off peritoneal infection.

eliminations.label: haustra - colonic sacculations, not a convergence point
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: haustra are the outpouchings of the colon created by the teniae. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: they are a feature along the colon, not a structure that converges on the appendix. ✅ Correct if: asked what creates the colon's segmented appearance.

eliminations.label: lesser omentum - upper abdomen
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the lesser omentum connects the liver to the lesser curvature of the stomach and duodenum. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is in the upper abdomen and unrelated to locating the appendix. ✅ Correct if: discussing the hepatogastric/hepatoduodenal ligaments (portal triad, omental foramen).

eliminations.label: psoas major - related to the psoas sign, not landmark identification
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the psoas major lies retroperitoneally; a retrocecal appendix overlying it produces a positive psoas sign. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it explains a physical-exam sign, not the intraoperative landmark used to find the appendiceal base. You chose this. ✅ Correct if: asked which muscle's irritation causes pain on hip extension (psoas sign).

reasoning: Migratory periumbilical -> RLQ pain + fever + leukocytosis = appendicitis
Surgeon must locate the appendix intraoperatively
Three teniae coli converge at the base of the appendix
Following the teniae locates the appendiceal base -> answer E
mechanism: The outer longitudinal smooth muscle of the cecum and colon is gathered into three bands - the teniae coli (mesocolic, omental, free). At the base of the vermiform appendix these three bands fuse into a continuous longitudinal coat. Surgically, tracing the teniae distally to their point of convergence reliably identifies the appendiceal base, which is fixed at the posteromedial cecum even when the appendix tip varies in position (retrocecal, pelvic, subcecal). This is more dependable than relying on the tip location or on surrounding structures.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Anatomy > GI: teniae coli converge at the appendiceal base; appendix arises from posteromedial cecum; McBurney point; appendiceal tip positions (retrocecal most common).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Recognize appendicitis quickly, then switch to the ANATOMY ask: an intraoperative landmark, not an exam sign. The teniae's convergence is that landmark.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on teniae coli, haustra, greater vs lesser omentum, McBurney point, psoas sign, retrocecal appendix? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: You chose D (psoas major). That underlies the psoas SIGN on exam, not the surgical landmark. To locate the appendix at operation, follow the teniae coli to their convergence.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Separate 'exam signs' (psoas, obturator, Rovsing) from 'surgical landmarks' (teniae convergence) so the question type tells you which to pick.

keywords.front: Intraoperative landmark to find the appendix
keywords.back: Teniae coli converge at the appendiceal base
Pain on hip extension - retrocecal appendix irritating psoas major

firstAid.detail: Anatomy: convergence at appendiceal base; cecal/colonic wall structure.
firstAid.location: Anatomy: convergence at appendiceal base; cecal/colonic wall structure.

firstAid.detail: GI/Surgery: McBurney point, migratory pain, psoas/obturator/Rovsing signs, leukocytosis.
firstAid.location: GI/Surgery: McBurney point, migratory pain, psoas/obturator/Rovsing signs, leukocytosis.

firstAid.detail: Anatomy: greater vs lesser omentum, mesentery, retroperitoneal psoas relations.
firstAid.location: Anatomy: greater vs lesser omentum, mesentery, retroperitoneal psoas relations.

memoryHook: Three teniae are roads that all lead to one town: the appendix base. Follow the roads to find it.
